title: Shotgun proteomics for archaeological bone material - in solution protocol for bone samples from tropical sites
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2rnus","text":"The analysis of bone ancient proteins (palaeoproteomics) is revolutionising the design of research strategies for zooarchaeological assemblages worldwide. By providing means to identify species presence in sites from samples that are not identifiable macroscopically through traditional methods, palaeoproteomics is offering a new exciting approach to understand people’s past interactions with their environments.\nNevertheless, this technique is not depleted of challenges.  Proteins preserved in ancient bones have undergone several modifications, most of which we do not know yet how they change the organic bone component. As a relatively new technique, much progress is needed in the optimization and development of\nlaboratory protocols tailored to different taphonomic histories.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"32p3r","text":"With a focus in European context, they are currently just few examples of the application of this technique in non-temperate contexts.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"crhhu","text":"This protocol presents a modified version of a common pipeline to prepare bone samples documented in tropical archaeological or palaeontological sites for LC MS/MS analysis. The main differences with other laboratory protocols consist of reducing washings steps, avoiding reduction/alkylation processes, optimizing protein digestion and concentrating eluted samples. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"brb37","text":"This protocol has been successfully tested at the Australian National University, obtaining positive results for archaeological bone samples from Northwest Australia, Timor-Leste, PNG and Indonesia.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"3v0eo","text":"1. Perform all steps in a standard wet chemistry laboratory, as a minimum biosafety level","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"347f3","text":"2. Wear PPE while conducting all the steps (laboratory coat, gloves, hairnet and safety googles)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7pr4m","text":"3. Be aware of any specific regulation in the handling, storage and disposal of reagents and chemical waste","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"bpc4d","text":"1. Reagents should be prepared and stored in glass labware only. We recommend using the same glass labware for every analysis to avoid external contamination by detergents or other chemicals","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2v1ng","text":"2. We recommend preparing reagents just before starting the protocol. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2ohol","text":"3. Handle reagents under a fume hood only","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2ussu","text":"4. Use ultrapure water for the preparation of reagents","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9ol9i","text":"5. Wipe down all working benches with  or  prior to begin working","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":37,"length":1},{"key":1,"offset":42,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"eba72","text":"6. All steps should be performed in a dedicated wet chemistry laboratory. If the presence of human remains is suspected, we recommend sampling in a DNA-free/P3 laboratory ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"reagents","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"id":182137,"guid":"41A991A3341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","name":"70% isopropyl alcohol","mol_weight":0,"sku":"","url":"http://None","cas_number":"","rrid":"","vendor":{"id":1,"name":"Contributed by users","link":"https://www.protocols.io","is_requested":false,"image":{"source":"/img/vendors/1.png","placeholder":"/img/vendors/1.png"}},"is_public":true,"can_edit":false}},"1":{"type":"reagents","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"id":4247,"guid":"4106C9E6341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","name":"70% Ethanol ","mol_weight":0,"sku":"","url":"","cas_number":"","rrid":"","vendor":{"id":1,"name":"Contributed by users","link":"https://www.protocols.io","is_requested":false,"image":{"source":"/img/vendors/1.png","placeholder":"/img/vendors/1.png"}},"is_public":true,"can_edit":false}}}}

steps: Sample extraction Use a pair of plyers to break apart a bone chip from bone sample or select a small bone fragment It is recommended to avoid drilling the specimens to obtain bone powder as the high speed of the drill head produces heat that can damage already degraded proteins To avoid unnecessary damage of archaeological specimens with no preserved proteins, a screening step using ATR-FTIR has been developed and will be published separately
Sample homogenising Grind manually with mortar and pestle until sample is homogenised into a fine powder

Sample weighing Weigh out 20 mg - 30 mg bone powder for each technical/biological duplicate Add bone powder to 1.5 mL LoBind eppendorf tube It is recommended to process technical duplicates (ideally triplicates) for each of the extracted samples
Add 500 µL of freshly made 0.6 Molarity (M) Hydrochloric acid Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) to each sample

Store samples at 4 °C until demineralisation is completed Demineralisation can take several days. When demineralised, bone becomes spongy and translucent Monitor the samples to avoid protein loss If demineralisation stops (no bubbling is visible) but the samples are not fully demineralised, exchange supernatant after centrifugation at 10000 x g, Room temperature, 10 minutes Prepare laboratory blanks at this step. Blanks should follow the same steps as the archaeological samples

When samples are demineralised, centrifuge samples at 10000 x g, Room temperature, 10 minutes to precipitate the extracted proteins to the base of the tube Extract supernatant and discard. If the acid soluble fraction wants to be analysed, store supernatant in a new tube at -20 °C Do not disturb bone pellet during pipetting

Acid washing Wash bone pellet with 500 µL of pH 8 50 millimolar (mM) Ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3) Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #AC393212500 Centrifuge at 10000 x g, Room temperature, 5 minutes Discard buffer Repeat the washing step until samples reach neutral pH Centrifuge (Benchtop Centrifuge, Eppendorf, 5405000441, Any benchtop centrifuge will suffice, https://online-shop.eppendorf.us/US-en/Centrifugation-44533/Centrifuges-44534/Centrifuge-5425-PF-243560.html )
Buffer exchange Add 100 µL of 50 millimolar (mM) AmBic to bone pellet

Gelatinization Incubate samples for 01:00:00 at 65 °C Samples will be hot! Take care when handling the samples ThermoMixer (Benchtop Incubator, Eppendorf, 5382000023, Any heat block will suffice, https://online-shop.eppendorf.us/US-en/Temperature-Control-and-Mixing-44518/Instruments-44519/Eppendorf-ThermoMixerC-PF-19703.html , https://s3.amazonaws.com/pr-journal/bcrsa.jpeg )
Sample cleaning Wipe the external surface of the bone with bleach 10% Bleach Contributed by users Remove the outer layer of the bone sample, either by polishing or manual removal
Decontamination Place open tubes under UV light for at least 00:15:00
Centrifuge samples at 10000 x g, Room temperature, 10 minutes
Transfer 50 µL of supernatant to each of the labelled 1.5 mL LoBind Eppendorf tubes If required, store samples that will not be use at -20 °C
Prepare two 1.5 mL LoBind Eppendorf tubes for each sample Label tubes with sample name/number followed by 'A' and 'B'

To calculate the amount of protease required to successfully digest your sample into peptides, use the protein assay method more suited for your samples and your plate reader Commercial protein assay kits provide user guides which describe the working procedures specific for each kit. In general, and based in our experience, BCA kits perform well for archaeological samples with low collagen/protein concentrations

There are several available Mass Spectrometry Grade Proteases, each following specific digestion procedures. Here we describe the common protocol used for Pierce Trypsin Protease Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #90057
Reconstitute lyophilised trypsin with 50 millimolar (mM) Glacial acetic acid Fisher Scientific Catalog #A38-500 (CAS: 64-19-7) to a concentration of 1 mg/mL
Add protease to a ratio of 1:20-1:100
Incubate samples Overnight at 400 rpm, 37 °C,
Stop digestion by freezing at -20 °C

Concentrate samples to dryness Samples can be concentrated in a Speedy Vacuum Concentration can take around 06:00:00 , depending on equipment, temperature, volume and other factors. Monitor samples carefully

Preparation Prepare conditioning, washing and elution solution (see Materials for details on reagents) Label three 0.5 mL tubes per sample. Each tube will contain conditioning solution ( 40 µL ), washing solution ( 70 µL ) and elution solution ( 30 µL ). Add an additional tube per sample for solvent waste
Pretreat sample Resuspend samples in 20 µL 0.1 % (v/v) Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) Bio Basic Inc. Catalog #TC8960.SIZE.100mL . Vortex thoroughly and sonicate for 00:10:00 to ensure resuspension
Condition tip Set pipettor at 10 µL and securely attach the tip Aspirate 10 µL of conditioning solution and discard solvent. Repeat. Keep pipette plunger depressed.
Equilibrate tip Aspirate 10 µL washing solution and discard. Repeat. Keep pipette plunger depressed.
Bind sample Aspirate up to 10 µL of pre-treated sample into tip. Dispense and aspirate for up to 10 cycles to improve binding. Dispense sample into a waste tube. Keep pipette plunger depressed
Purify/desalt Aspirate 10 µL of washing solution and discard solvent. Repeat 3 times. Keep pipette plunger depressed.

Elute Elute with 10 µL 0.1 % (v/v) Formic acid, LC-MS grade Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #28905 in 75 % (v/v) Acetonitrile (ACN) Fischer Scientific Catalog #10660131 . Dispense in glass vial LC/MS-grade
Concentrate samples in Speedy Vacuum until dry
Resuspend samples in 30 µL of 5 % (v/v) ACN in 0.1 % (v/v) Formic Acid

Sonicate for 00:10:00 and mix thoroughly making sure there are no bubbles on the bottom of the vial insert Samples are now ready to be injected into the LC MS/MS instrument. If not analysing immediately, store samples in 4 °C (1-2 days) or -20 °C (long storage)
creator: Sofia Samper Carro
